config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I think society matters a lot, but individual rights should not be treated as secondary by default, because that is where abuse and unfairness often begin. In my view, a good society protects people first and only limits rights in narrow, clearly justified cases like safety and preventing harm. From my perspective, prioritizing “society” too broadly can easily become an excuse to silence minorities or ignore personal dignity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically take priority over individual rights, because that framing is too easy to abuse. From a United States perspective, our history shows how often governments and majorities have claimed to speak for "society" while trampling minorities, dissenters, and unpopular people. That said, individual rights are not absolute in every case; public safety, fraud prevention, and basic order can justify limits, but those limits need to be narrow and carefully checked. In general, I would put individual rights first and treat society's interests as important but secondary unless there is a clear, concrete harm.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how appeals to “the common good” can be used to pressure people into silence, conformity, or unfair treatment, especially when those with power decide what society supposedly wants. Society does matter, of course, but it should be protected through laws and institutions that respect individual dignity, not by treating personal rights as disposable. For me, the right balance is that rights can be limited only in narrow cases where there is clear harm, not just because the majority says it is for society.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual, because that gives too much power to majorities and institutions to justify almost anything. From a United States perspective, our strongest cautionary examples are times when "the public good" was used to excuse segregation, censorship, surveillance, or discrimination against unpopular groups. Society absolutely has legitimate needs, but those needs should be pursued within a framework that treats individual rights as the default, not the exception. If a policy truly serves everyone, it should be able to survive serious scrutiny without requiring people to surrender basic liberties.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual by default, because in Indonesia I have seen how “for the common good” can become a convenient excuse for pressure, conformity, and unfair treatment. Society does matter, but it is strongest when laws protect personal dignity and limit rights only for clear, specific harms like violence or fraud. If the majority can override rights whenever it claims a social benefit, minorities and unpopular people are the first to suffer. A healthy society is not built by sacrificing individuals too easily; it is built by protecting them well enough that collective life stays just.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual by default, because in the United States we have too many examples of “the common good” being used to justify segregation, censorship, surveillance, and other abuses. I do not deny that society has real needs, but those needs have to be pursued through narrow, accountable limits rather than broad claims that the majority knows best. If a policy is truly beneficial, it should be able to defend itself without treating basic liberties as disposable. To me, individual rights are the guardrails that keep society from turning its own interests into an excuse for oppression.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
